sourceTitle: Create streamlined project management workflows using database automations

questions.question: What are the two core components of a Notion database automation?

options.explanation: Automations are built from a trigger event and the sequence of actions that follow.
Filters and views are ways to display data, not automation components.
Templates and blocks help create content, but they are not part of automation logic.
Permissions control access, not automation flow.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a benefit of using database automations for project management?

options.explanation: Automation notifies team members, cutting idle time.
Automation handles repetitive manual steps, lowering mistakes.

options.text: Eliminating the need for any human oversight
options.explanation: Automation assists, but human oversight is still required for quality and decision‑making.
Automation lets you keep workflows inside Notion, avoiding third‑party tools.

questions.question: When creating a new automation, which of the following trigger options can you choose?

options.explanation: One of the basic trigger types is "Page added".

options.text: When a user logs in to Notion
options.explanation: User login is not a database property change, so it cannot be a trigger.

options.text: When a comment is posted on any page
options.explanation: Comments are not a database property; they cannot directly trigger automations.
Workspace creation is outside the scope of a single database automation.

questions.question: In the "Kick off a project" automation example, what property change serves as the trigger?

options.explanation: The automation fires when the project's status moves from Not started to Planning.
Editing the name is not the trigger described in the example.

options.text: A new team member is added to the project
options.explanation: Adding a member is not part of this specific automation.

options.text: The project deadline is set to today
options.explanation: The trigger is based on status, not on the deadline property.

questions.question: Which action is NOT mentioned as a possible result of a database automation?

options.text: Adding a new page to another database
options.explanation: Automation can create pages in another database.
Slack notifications are a listed action.

options.text: Generating a PDF report and attaching it to the page
options.explanation: The article does not mention PDF generation as a built‑in action.
Updating properties is a core automation capability.

questions.question: When using formula‑based automations, what is the purpose of the "Define variables" step?

options.text: To create custom calculations that can be referenced in later actions
options.explanation: Defining variables lets you store formula results for use in actions such as email content.

options.text: To set the automation's schedule (e.g., run daily)
options.explanation: Scheduling is not handled by defining variables.

options.text: To assign permissions for who can edit the automation
options.explanation: Permissions are managed elsewhere, not via variables.

options.text: To generate a random ID for the new page
options.explanation: Random IDs are not the purpose of defining variables.

questions.question: In the email‑on‑form‑submission automation, which trigger type should you select?

options.explanation: When a form submission creates a new page, the "Page added" trigger fires the email action.
The example uses a new page, not an edited property, as the trigger.
Deletion does not correspond to a form submission.
Comments are not part of the described automation.

questions.question: Why might a team choose to use a formula‑based automation instead of a simple trigger‑action pair?

options.text: To combine data from multiple databases or compute dynamic values before an action runs
options.explanation: Formulas enable cross‑database lookups and calculations, which simple triggers cannot do.

options.text: Because formulas run faster than standard actions
options.explanation: Speed is not the primary advantage; functionality is.

options.text: To avoid paying for a Notion paid plan
options.explanation: Automation availability depends on the plan, not on using formulas.

options.text: To automatically create a new workspace for each task
options.explanation: Workspaces cannot be created via formulas; this is outside the scope of database automations.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the availability of database automations is correct?

options.text: They are only available on certain paid plan types
options.explanation: The article notes that automations are limited to specific plan tiers.

options.text: They are available for free on all Notion accounts
options.explanation: Free accounts do not have access to automation features.

options.text: They require a separate third‑party integration to function
options.explanation: Automations are built‑in; third‑party tools are optional.

options.text: They can only be used in personal workspaces, not team workspaces
options.explanation: Automations are designed for team collaboration as well as personal use.

questions.question: In the "Start a new task" automation example, which property is automatically set to "Not Started"?

options.explanation: The automation sets the task's status property to "Not Started" when a page is added.
Priority is not mentioned as being set in this example.
The due date is not automatically assigned in this automation.
Project name is not part of the described property updates.
